g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the completed "Walker" robot. The robot has a long, narrow wooden body supported by four distinct legs.
The Body: The central chassis is contoured, with a wide rear section and a narrower "neck" leading to the front.
The Legs: Each of the four legs has a jointed appearance, consisting of an upper thigh-like piece and a lower shin-piece that ends in a small circular foot. The legs are angled outward, giving the robot a stable, wide stance.
The Head: At the very front of the body, the ultrasonic range finder (the "eyes") is mounted vertically. It is held in place by the same wooden holder and O-ring system used in the previous build, facing forward to detect obstacles.
Overall Aesthetic: Unlike the boxy "Base Station" chassis, this robot has a more biological, animal-like silhouette. The drawing is white line art on a blue grid background.
Tactile & Structural Landmarks The Walker introduces more complex mechanical joints. Here is what a student can feel for to orient themselves:
The Quadruped Stance: A student can identify this robot by feeling for the four separate leg assemblies. Unlike the solid side panels of the previous robot, these legs are thin and movable.
Front vs. Back: The "front" of the robot is easily identified by the protruding ultrasonic sensor eyes. The "back" of the body is wider and has a rounded tail-like edge.
Jointed Feet: Each leg ends in a small, smooth wooden circle. These are the contact points that touch the ground.
Contoured Spine: The main body (the "spine") is not a flat plate; it has curved edges that dip in the middle and rise toward the head and tail, making it feel more organic than the rectangular baseplate.
g.title: Walker: Assembly Overview

g.desc: Parts list for the Walker Kit
g.title: Inventory

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the preparation of the robot's legs.
The Action: An orange dashed arrow shows a Grommet (a small, spool-shaped rubber piece) being inserted into the circular cutout at the bottom of a wooden Leg.
The Instruction: "Repeat 4 times to install Grommets onto each Leg."
Detail Inset: A square close-up box shows a cross-section of the finished foot. The wooden leg sits in the center "valley" of the grommet, with the two rubber "wheels" of the grommet sandwiching the wood to hold it securely.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "1" is displayed, and the first of five circles is filled in.
Assembly Tips: This step creates the "tires" for your robot. Since the Walker needs to grip the floor to move, getting these right is key:
The Grommet Shape: A grommet feels like a tiny rubber tire with a deep groove running around the middle. Think of it like a miniature spool of thread.
The Snug Fit: You will need to squeeze the rubber slightly to pop it into the hole at the bottom of the leg. Once it's in, the wood should sit right in that middle groove.
Consistency Check: After doing this four times, line up all four legs. The rubber "feet" should be identical on all of them. If one feels wobbly, the grommet might not be fully seated in the notch.
Tactile Landmark: The top of the leg has a much more complex shape with notches and a square cutout—make sure you are putting the grommet into the smooth, circular hole at the bottom (the "ankle").
g.title: Walker Step 1: Foot Assembly

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing how to prepare a motor attachment.
The Components: A large, rectangular wooden piece with notched ends (the Spline Horn) is shown above a small, oval plastic piece with three protruding cylinders (the Servo Horn).
The Action: Two small screws are shown being driven through the circular holes in the wooden Spline Horn to secure it to the plastic Servo Horn below.
The Instruction: "Repeat 2 times to attach Spline Horn to each Servo Horn with Screws."
Landmark: The letter "D" is highlighted in a square box next to one of the screws, identifying the specific screw type from the kit.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "2" is displayed, and two of the five circles are filled in.
Assembly Tips This step requires precision, as these parts translate the motor's rotation into the robot's walking motion:
Identifying the "Horn": The plastic Servo Horn is a small, white or black oval piece. It has a "splined" (toothed) hole in the center that will eventually press-fit onto the motor's metal shaft.
Symmetry is Key: The wooden Spline Horn must be centered on the plastic horn. If it is crooked, the robot might have a "limp" when it walks.
Screw Type "D": These are likely very small, short screws designed to bite into plastic. Be careful not to over-tighten them, as it’s easy to strip the plastic holes.
The Goal: After repeating this twice, you will have two identical "motor arms" ready to be attached to the robot's body or legs.
g.title: Walker Step 2A: Servo Horn Assembly

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing how to attach the legs to the wooden Spline Horn.
The Components: The horizontal wooden Spline Horn (from Step 2A) is shown above two wooden Legs (from Step 1).
The Action: Two long screws, identified by the letter "A" in a square box, are driven through the holes at the ends of the Spline Horn. They pass through the notched tops of the legs and are secured with hex nuts.
The Joint Detail: A square inset box shows a cross-section of the T-slot connection, where the nut is held inside the wooden leg's notched top as the screw is tightened from above.
Warnings: A circular exclamation icon at the bottom right notes: "Make sure the Spline Horn is facing downwards." This refers to the side with the plastic servo horn attached.
The Instruction: "Attach Legs to Servo Horn with Screws and Nuts. Repeat 2 times with all four legs"
Assembly Tips: This is the first time you are creating a "hinge" for the Walker. Pay close attention to the orientation so the robot walks forward rather than in circles:
Screw Type "A": These are the longest screws in your kit. They need that extra length to pass through both the Spline Horn and the thickest part of the leg.
The T-Slot Feel: Just like the Base Station chassis, you’ll need to slide a hex nut into the little "T" shaped cutout at the top of each leg. Use your thumb to hold the nut in place while you turn the screw from the top.
Orientation Check: The "Spline Horn facing downwards" rule is critical. If you feel the plastic oval piece from Step 2A, it should be on the bottom side of the wooden arm, facing toward the ground.
Leg Placement: Ensure the legs are pointing the same direction (with the rubber grommet "feet" at the bottom). Once finished, you should have two separate assemblies, each looking like a pair of legs connected by a wooden bridge.
g.title: Walker Step 2B: Attaching Legs

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the assembly of the robot's main body.
The Components: Two long, contoured wooden pieces called Spines are being joined to a flat horizontal base.
The Action: Orange dashed arrows indicate that the Spines slide vertically into two parallel sets of rectangular slots located on the base.
The Structure: Once inserted, the Spines stand upright and parallel to each other, forming the vertical walls of the robot's body.
Instruction: "Install the Spines on both the Torso and Trunk".
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "3" is displayed, and three out of five circles are filled in.
Assembly Tips: This step creates the central frame that holds the motors and electronics. Here is how to verify the fit by touch:
Identifying the "Spines": These are the longest wooden pieces in the Walker kit. They have a distinct "hunched" or curved top edge that mimics an animal's back.
Torso vs. Trunk: The Torso is the front section (where the "eyes" will eventually go), and the Trunk is the rear section. The baseplate has specific slots for both.
The Slot Alignment: You will feel four rectangular holes on the baseplate. Each Spine has tabs on the bottom that must line up perfectly with these holes.
Symmetry Check: Once installed, the two Spines should be exactly the same height and distance apart. If one feels tilted, check that the tabs are fully pushed through the baseplate.
g.title: Walker Step 3: Spine Installation
Walker Step 4A: Servo Installation
g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing how to mount a servomotor to the robot's body.
The Components: A rectangular servomotor (the motor) is shown being sandwiched between the flat baseplate and a square wooden Servo Mount piece.
The Action: Two screws, identified by the letter "C" in a square box, pass upward through the Servo Mount and the baseplate. They are secured on the top side with two hex nuts that sit against the parallel Spines.
Critical Orientation: An orange arrow on the motor points away from the center of the robot, paired with the instruction: "Install Torso and Trunk Servos so motors are facing OUTWARDS".
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "4" is displayed, and four out of five circles are filled in.
Assembly Tips: Installing the motors correctly is the most important part of ensuring the Walker can actually move.
The "Facing Outwards" Rule: Feel for the rotating white plastic shaft (the output spline) on the motor. This shaft must point away from the robot's "spine" toward the left or right side. If the shafts face each other, the legs will be trapped inside the body.
Screw Type "C": These are medium-length screws. You will need to hold the hex nut in place on top of the baseplate while turning the screw from the bottom.
The Servo Sandwich: You are creating a layer: first the screw head, then the wooden Servo Mount, then the baseplate, and finally the hex nut. The motor body itself sits snugly in the rectangular cutout of the baseplate.
Repeat for Both Ends: You will need to perform this exact assembly at both the front (Torso) and the back (Trunk) of the robot.

A technical blueprint showing how to attach the leg pairs to the servomotors on the underside of the robot.
The Action: The two leg assemblies (from Step 2B) are shown being pressed onto the motor shafts at the front and back of the baseplate. Two screws, identified by the letter "B" in a square box, are driven into the center of the plastic servo horns to lock the legs in place.
Note on Anatomy: A "NOTE" box on the right features a silhouette of a dog with highlighted back legs. The text reads: "The ‘knees’ should face towards the tail of Walker, like a dog."
Warning: A circular exclamation icon at the top center warns: "Do not overtighten the screws."
The View: The diagram shows the robot from an underside perspective to clearly show the motor connections.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips: This step defines the "stance" of your Walker. Because you are working with movable motor parts, keep these tactile cues in mind:
The "Knee" Orientation: Feel the shape of the legs. They have a slight bend or "joint." To follow the dog-like anatomy mentioned in the note, ensure that the bend of the legs points toward the rear (the wider part of the chassis).
Aligning the Splines: The plastic horn in the center of your leg assembly has tiny teeth (splines). You will feel a slight "click" or "drop" when those teeth line up with the teeth on the motor shaft. Don't force it—rotate it slightly until they mesh.
Screw Type "B": These are specifically designed for the motor shafts. They are usually very thin.
The "Goldilocks" Tightness: The warning about overtightening is key. If the screw is too tight, it might put pressure on the motor casing and prevent it from turning. It should be "finger-tight"—just enough so the legs don't wobble or fall off.
Symmetry Check: Once both pairs are on, set the robot on its feet. It should stand level. If one pair of legs is angled drastically differently than the other, you may need to pull them off the spline and re-seat them at a better angle.
g.title: Walker Step 4B: Mounting the Leg Assemblies

g.desc:
A technical blueprint showing the assembly of the ultrasonic range finder.
The Components: The rectangular range finder circuit board (with its two circular "eye" sensors) is shown alongside a uniquely notched wooden Holder.
The Action: A black O-ring (rubber band) is shown looping around the back of the circuit board. Orange dashed arrows indicate that the O-ring stretches to hook into the small notches on the left and right sides of the wooden holder.
Instruction: "Use O-Ring to attach Range Finder to Holder."
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "5" is displayed, and all five circles are now filled in.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Even if a student has done this before, the Walker's orientation is slightly different. Here’s how to ensure it’s ready for the final mission:
The Rubber Tension: A student should feel the O-ring snap securely into the two side notches of the holder. If it feels loose, the sensor might vibrate while the robot walks, which can cause "jittery" distance readings.
The "Eyes" Orientation: Ensure the metallic cylinders are facing outward, away from the wooden piece. The wooden holder has a curved "bridge" that should sit just below the sensors.
Cross-Build Consistency: This is the exact same sensor assembly used in the first robot. If a student is disassembling the Base Station to build the Walker, they may already have this sub-assembly completed and ready to go.
g.title: Walker Step 5A: Sensor Assembly

g.desc:
A technical line-art blueprint showing the final mounting of the sensor unit.
The Action: The completed Range Finder and Holder assembly (from Step 5A) is shown hovering above the front of the robot.
The Connection: An orange dashed arrow points from the bottom tab of the wooden holder down into a square slot on the front of the horizontal baseplate.
Placement: Two circular holes are visible on either side of this square slot, intended for the screws that will lock it in place.
The Instruction: "Insert Holder into Walker torso."
Tactile & Alignment Tips Now that the robot has its "eyes," a student can use these tactile markers to confirm everything is in the right place:
Identifying the "Torso": The torso is the front end of the baseplate. You can identify it by the square cutout located centrally between the two front legs.
The "Tongue and Groove" Fit: The bottom of the wooden holder has a small rectangular protrusion (a tab). This should slide snugly into the square slot on the baseplate.
Orientation Check: Make sure the two metallic sensor "eyes" are facing forward, away from the robot's body.
The Screw Holes: Once the tab is inserted, the two small horizontal notches at the base of the holder should line up with the two circular holes on the baseplate. You will likely use the same screw-and-nut method seen in the Base Station build to finalize this connection.
g.title: Walker Step 5B: Attaching the Range Finder

g.desc:
A close-up technical blueprint showing the final fastening of the sensor unit.
The Components: The range finder and holder (inserted in Step 5B) are shown seated on the front of the baseplate.
The Action: Two screws, labeled with a square box containing the letter "A", are driven upward from the bottom of the baseplate. They pass through the horizontal base and are secured on the top side by two hex nuts.
Hardware Detail: The letter "A" specifies the use of 3/8" 4-40 screws.
Alignment: Orange dashed arrows show the screws aligning with the two circular holes flanking the central square slot where the holder was inserted.
Tactile & Mechanical Tips Since this is the final bit of "heavy lifting" for the chassis, here is how to make sure the Walker's "eyes" are steady:
Identifying Screw Type "A": These are your 3/8" 4-40 screws. By touch, these are the long, slender screws that provide enough length to pass through both the baseplate and the flange of the wooden sensor holder.
Nut Placement: This is a tight space. Use one finger to hold the hex nut over the hole on the top side of the baseplate while you use a screwdriver from underneath to thread the screw into it.
Rigidity Test: Once tightened, give the sensor holder a gentle wiggle. It should feel completely rigid and integrated with the main body. If it wobbles, your Walker's "vision" will be blurry (mathematically speaking) once it starts walking.
Final Form: Your Walker now has a complete body, four legs with rubber feet, two "muscle" motors, and a forward-facing sensor.
g.title: Walker Step 5C: Securing the Distance Sensor

g.desc:
A final technical illustration titled "ASSEMBLY COMPLETE!" in large, bold white letters. The graphic shows the finished Walker robot from an isometric perspective.
The Final Form: The robot stands on its four legs with rubber grommet feet. The long, contoured spine pieces are visible, and the ultrasonic range finder "eyes" are securely mounted at the front.
Details: All joints, servomotors, and fasteners from the previous steps are shown integrated into a single, cohesive mechanical unit.
Call to Action: Below the robot, the text reads: "Head to Piper Make to continue your mission." ---
Final Mechanical Audit Before moving to the computer, a student can perform this quick "physical checkup" to ensure the Walker is ready to move:
The "Table Test": Set the robot on a flat surface. All four rubber feet should touch the ground simultaneously. If it wobbles, check the angle of the legs on the motor splines.
Joint Clearance: Gently move the legs by hand (be careful not to force the motors too hard). Ensure the wooden legs don't rub against the side of the chassis or the sensor holder.
The "Head" Shake: Give the ultrasonic sensor a tiny poke. It should be rock-solid thanks to those Type A screws.
Wire Management: Ensure the wires coming out of the two servomotors and the range finder are clear of the leg's range of motion. You don't want the robot to accidentally trip over its own "nervous system"!
g.title: Walker: Assembly Complete!

g.desc: make.playpiper.com with an arrow pointed to the right, indicating that you are ready to continue onto the website to program your Walker Robot.
g.title: Go to make.playpiper.com to continue
